Party_Details.Name: Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd held an extraordinary general meeting on 27 January 2001.
At the extraordinary general meeting on 27 January 2001, an ordinary resolution was passed to remove Koh Guan Poh as a director of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd.
During the extraordinary general meeting on 27 January 2001, Polymotor Pte Ltd cast a vote through a representative, Loh Song Huat.
Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd filed an application seeking a court declaration that Koh Guan Poh ceased to be a director as of 27 January 2001.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the ordinary resolution passed at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27 validly removed Koh Guan Poh from his position as director, having regard to the manner in which Polymotor Pte Ltd was represented at that meeting and the use of a written resolution under Article 92.
Party_Details.Rule: A company may remove a director by an ordinary resolution passed at a properly convened general meeting, and where the company’s articles allow resolutions in writing signed by a majority of directors to be valid as if passed at a duly called board meeting, such written resolutions must comply with the requirements of company law and the articles, including proper participation procedures for directors.
Party_Details.Application: Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd relied on an ordinary resolution passed at its extraordinary general meeting on 2001-01-27 to treat Koh Guan Poh as removed from his position as director. Representation of the shareholder Polymotor Pte Ltd at that meeting depended on a written resolution signed by a majority of Polymotor’s directors under an article in similar terms to Article 92, and the effectiveness of that written resolution affected whether the shareholder representation and resulting vote at the extraordinary general meeting aligned with the company’s constitutional and procedural framework.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court recorded that no remedy was sought against the first, third, fourth and fifth defendants and summarily dismissed the application as against those defendants. The second defendant, Koh Guan Poh, contested the application on the ground that Polymotor Pte Ltd was not properly represented at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27 when a resolution was purportedly passed voting him out of office. The second defendant argued that on 2000-12-13 three of the four directors of Polymotor Pte Ltd, without notice to him, signed a written resolution appointing one of them, Loh Song Huat, as the representative of Polymotor Pte Ltd at any meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd, and that this written resolution was invalid in equity because he had no notice of it. The judgment set out legal propositions on directors as fiduciaries, the requirement that directors act bona fide in the interests of the company as a whole, and that lack of bona fides can invalidate a written resolution. It stated that directors collectively owe duties to the company and that no director or group of directors can exclude another director from deliberations or from contributing before a decision is taken, and that a meeting of the majority without notice to the minority is ineffective. The court referred to authorities including Chua Boon Chin v JM McCormack, Howard Smith v Ampol Petroleum, Lee Tak Samuel v Chou Wen Hsien, Re Portuguese Consolidated Copper Mines, Young v Ladies' Imperial Club, and Chan Choon Ming v Low Poh Choon. In discussing Chan Choon Ming v Low Poh Choon, the judgment noted that an article allowing written resolutions signed by a majority of directors must be read in the context that powers are conferred on directors collectively as a board, and that notice of an intended resolution must be given to every member of the board. The judgment quoted VC George J to the effect that without such notice, a controlling group could manage the company as a clique, leaving the minority in the dark, and that each such resolution without notice to the minority director was ineffective. The judgment also cited commentary from Walter Woon’s Company Law that even if articles allow a circular resolution to be effective when signed by the majority, notice must still be given to all directors to avoid a cabal of controlling directors acting without the knowledge of the rest of the board. The court then referred to equitable principles that a party seeking equitable relief must come with clean hands and that, when exercising a discretionary remedy, the court may lift the corporate veil and scrutinise the real motives of those behind the company. Applying these principles, the judgment characterised the act of the majority directors of Polymotor Pte Ltd in signing the written resolution appointing one of them as corporate representative as a selfish and surreptitious act to achieve a private agenda of discarding the second defendant as a director of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd. It observed that this conduct might amount to conspiracy if the second defendant suffered damage and that the second defendant had also described it as minority oppression or injustice, though the court did not decide those issues. The judgment noted that the majority directors acted without giving notice to the second defendant, who was also a substantial shareholder of Polymotor Pte Ltd and was away in Thailand at the time, and concluded that the third defendant was not a true representative of Polymotor Pte Ltd. The court described the act of the majority directors as substantial injustice and lacking good faith, and held that Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd had notice of the defect because those who voted to remove the second defendant were aware of the defect and unlawfulness of the written resolution. The court further held that Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd, in seeking equitable remedies of declaration and injunction, did not come with clean hands, and distinguished the facts from Lee Tak Samuel on the basis of procedural irregularity and lack of good faith. On this reasoning, the court dismissed the plaintiffs’ application against the second defendant and ordered the plaintiffs to pay the costs of the application, recording the outcome as ‘Application dismissed’.

Party_Details.Name: Lim Heng Lee

Facts.Text: Lim Heng Lee was a shareholder associated with the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd held on 27 January 2001.
Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd initiated an Originating Summons naming Lim Heng Lee as the first defendant, but did not seek any substantive remedy against him.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether any relief should be granted against Lim Heng Lee in relation to the resolution passed at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27.
Party_Details.Rule: Relief in company law proceedings is granted only where the claimant establishes a basis for orders against the particular party in respect of the impugned corporate acts.
Party_Details.Application: Lim Heng Lee was a party associated with the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27, although the corporate actions in question primarily concerned the status of another director and representation by Polymotor Pte Ltd.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The judgment recorded that at the hearing it was obvious that no remedy was sought against the first, third, fourth and fifth defendants and that the application was summarily dismissed as against those four defendants, including Lim Heng Lee.

Party_Details.Name: Koh Guan Poh

Facts.Text: Koh Guan Poh objected to the validity of the representation of Polymotor Pte Ltd at the extraordinary general meeting.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the resolution passed at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27 was effective to terminate Koh Guan Poh’s position as director, given the manner in which Polymotor Pte Ltd was represented and the use of a written resolution by its directors.
Party_Details.Rule: The effectiveness of a shareholder’s vote at a company meeting depends on the shareholder being represented in accordance with the company’s constitution and applicable company law, including any requirements governing written resolutions of the shareholder’s board of directors and participation of all directors entitled to be involved in such decisions.
Party_Details.Application: Koh Guan Poh’s status as director of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd after 2001-01-27 turned on whether Polymotor Pte Ltd’s representative at the extraordinary general meeting was appointed through a written resolution that complied with the relevant article permitting written resolutions and with principles governing director participation in board decisions, and whether the vote cast on behalf of Polymotor Pte Ltd could therefore be treated as a valid expression of that shareholder’s wishes.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court accepted the second defendant’s challenge to the effectiveness of the written resolution used to appoint Polymotor Pte Ltd’s representative and to the resulting vote at the extraordinary general meeting. It held that directors, as fiduciaries, must act bona fide in the interests of the company as a whole and that lack of bona fides is a ground to invalidate a written resolution. The judgment emphasised that directors collectively owe duties to the company and that no director or group of directors may exclude another director from deliberations or from contributing before a decision is taken. Relying on authorities such as Chan Choon Ming v Low Poh Choon, the court reasoned that even where articles allow a written resolution signed by a majority of directors to be effective, notice of the intended resolution must be given to all directors; otherwise, a controlling group could manage the company as a clique, leaving the minority in the dark. The court concluded that the majority directors of Polymotor Pte Ltd had acted in a selfish and surreptitious manner by signing the written resolution appointing one of them as corporate representative without notifying the second defendant, who was also a substantial shareholder and was overseas at the time. It regarded this conduct as substantial injustice and lacking good faith, and held that the third defendant was not a true representative of Polymotor Pte Ltd. The court found that Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd had notice of the defect because those who voted to remove the second defendant were aware of the defect and unlawfulness of the written resolution. It further held that Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd did not come to equity with clean hands in seeking declaratory and injunctive relief. On this basis, the court dismissed the application against the second defendant and ordered the plaintiffs to pay the costs of the application.

Party_Details.Name: Loh Song Huat

Facts.Text: At the material time, Loh Song Huat was a shareholder of Polymotor Pte Ltd.
At the material time, Loh Song Huat was a director of Polymotor Pte Ltd.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the written resolution of 2000-12-13 appointing Loh Song Huat as Polymotor Pte Ltd’s representative at meetings of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd was effective for the purposes of the vote at the extraordinary general meeting on 2001-01-27.
Party_Details.Rule: Where a company’s articles allow a resolution in writing signed by a majority of directors to be as valid as a resolution passed at a duly convened board meeting, the validity of such a written resolution depends on compliance with the article and with principles requiring that directors entitled to participate are not improperly excluded from the decision-making process.
Party_Details.Application: The effectiveness of the 2000-12-13 written resolution appointing Loh Song Huat as Polymotor Pte Ltd’s representative determined whether he could cast Polymotor Pte Ltd’s vote at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27 in a manner consistent with Polymotor Pte Ltd’s internal governance and the requirements for written resolutions by its board.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court held that the written resolution appointing Loh Song Huat as Polymotor Pte Ltd’s representative was ineffective because it was signed by a majority of directors without notice to the second defendant, who was also a director and substantial shareholder. Applying the principles from Chan Choon Ming v Low Poh Choon and related authorities, the court reasoned that even where articles permit written resolutions signed by a majority, notice must be given to all directors to prevent a controlling group from acting as a clique. The court described the conduct of the majority directors as selfish and surreptitious, undertaken to advance a private agenda of removing the second defendant from the board of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd, and concluded that the third defendant was not a true representative of Polymotor Pte Ltd for the purposes of the vote at the extraordinary general meeting. As no substantive relief was sought against the third defendant, the application was summarily dismissed as against him.

Party_Details.Name: Lim Soo Chong

Facts.Text: At the material time, Loh Song Huat was a shareholder of Polymotor Pte Ltd.
At the material time, Loh Song Huat was a director of Polymotor Pte Ltd.
Loh Song Huat was one of the three directors who signed a written resolution appointing himself as the corporate representative of Polymotor Pte Ltd.
Loh Song Huat attended the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd as the purported representative of Polymotor Pte Ltd and participated in the vote.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the participation of Lim Soo Chong in signing the 2000-12-13 written resolution of Polymotor Pte Ltd had any effect on the validity of Polymotor Pte Ltd’s representation at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27.
Party_Details.Rule: Directors who participate in a written resolution must do so in a manner consistent with company law principles governing collective decision-making, including the requirement that other directors entitled to participate are not improperly excluded from notice of the resolution.
Party_Details.Application: Lim Soo Chong’s signature on the 2000-12-13 written resolution formed part of the majority used to appoint a representative for Polymotor Pte Ltd, and the validity of that appointment depended on whether the process respected the participation rights of all directors, including those not involved in signing.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court treated the written resolution signed by the majority directors of Polymotor Pte Ltd, including Lim Soo Chong, as ineffective because it was made without notice to the second defendant. It characterised the conduct of the majority directors as a selfish and surreptitious act aimed at removing the second defendant from the board of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd and held that the resulting appointment of the corporate representative was invalid. As no substantive relief was sought against the fourth defendant, the application was summarily dismissed as against him.

Party_Details.Name: Polymotor Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: At the material time, Polymotor Pte Ltd had five shareholders: Lim Soo San, Lim Soo Tiah, Lim Soo Chong, Koh Guan Poh, and Loh Song Huat.
At the material time, four of the shareholders of Polymotor Pte Ltd were also directors: Lim Soo Tiah, Lim Soo Chong, Koh Guan Poh, and Loh Song Huat.

On 13 December 2000 three of the four directors without notice to Koh Guan Poh signed a written resolution appointing one of them, Loh Song Huat, as the representative of Polymotor at any meeting of the plaintiffs.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether Polymotor Pte Ltd was properly represented at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27 for the purpose of voting on the resolution concerning the directorship of Koh Guan Poh.
Party_Details.Rule: A corporate shareholder is properly represented at a company meeting when its representative is appointed in accordance with its own constitutional documents and applicable company law, including any requirements for valid written resolutions of its board of directors.
Party_Details.Application: Polymotor Pte Ltd’s representation at the extraordinary general meeting of Polybuilding (S) Pte Ltd on 2001-01-27 depended on the validity of the 2000-12-13 written resolution by which three of its four directors appointed a representative, and on whether that process complied with the internal governance rules and participation rights of all directors.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court concluded that Polymotor Pte Ltd was not properly represented at the extraordinary general meeting because the written resolution appointing its representative was ineffective, having been signed by a majority of directors without notice to the second defendant. It held that the impropriety of the majority directors would be imputed to the company and that the appointment of the representative was invalid. As no substantive relief was sought against Polymotor Pte Ltd, the application was summarily dismissed as against the fifth defendant.
